title: Networking Basics (ip, netstat, curl, ssh)
summary: Understand fundamental networking concepts on Linux. Learn to inspect network interfaces, test connectivity, make HTTP requests, and connect securely to remote servers with SSH. These skills are critical for managing cloud infrastructure.

resources.title: SSH Crash Course - Authentication and More

questions.questionText: Which command is used to display network interface information on modern Linux?
questions.explanation: The 'ip' command (specifically 'ip addr' or 'ip a') is the modern replacement for ifconfig. It shows all network interfaces and their IP addresses.
The '-I' flag (or --head) tells curl to fetch only the HTTP headers, not the response body. This is useful for checking status codes and server information.

questions.questionText: What is the default port for SSH?
questions.explanation: SSH runs on port 22 by default. This can be changed in the SSH server configuration (/etc/ssh/sshd_config) for security purposes.

questions.questionText: Which command tests if a remote host is reachable?
questions.explanation: The 'ping' command sends ICMP echo request packets to a host and reports the response time. It is the simplest way to test network connectivity.
The 'ss' command (socket statistics) with flags: -t (TCP), -l (listening), -n (numeric ports), -p (process info) shows all TCP ports being listened on and which process is using them.

options.text: Listening TCP ports with process information

tasks.title: Network Diagnostics
tasks.description:
1. View your IP address: `ip addr show` (or `ip a`)
2. View the routing table: `ip route`
3. Test connectivity: `ping -c 4 google.com`
4. Trace the route to a host: `traceroute google.com` (install if needed)
5. DNS lookup: `nslookup google.com` and `dig google.com`
6. View listening ports: `ss -tlnp`
7. Make an HTTP request: `curl -v https://httpbin.org/get`
8. Fetch only headers: `curl -I https://httpbin.org/get`
9. Download a file: `curl -O https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jlevy/the-art-of-command-line/master/README.md`
10. Test a specific port: `curl -v telnet://google.com:80`
hints: If traceroute is not installed, try: sudo apt install traceroute
curl -v (verbose) shows the full HTTP request and response headers

tasks.title: SSH Key Setup and Remote Connection
tasks.description:
1. Generate an SSH key pair: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C 'your_email@example.com'`
2. View the public key: `cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub`
3. Start the SSH agent: `eval $(ssh-agent -s)`
4. Add your key: `ssh-add ~/.ssh/id_ed25519`
5. Set up an SSH config file:
   ```
   touch ~/.ssh/config
   chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config
   ```
6. Add a sample host entry:
   ```
   Host myserver
     HostName 192.168.1.100
     User devops
     IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_ed25519
   ```
7. If you have access to a remote server or VM, copy your key: `ssh-copy-id user@hostname`
8. Connect: `ssh user@hostname` (or `ssh myserver` if using the config)
hints: ed25519 keys are newer and more secure than RSA keys
If you do not have a remote server, you can practice with a free sandbox at killercoda.com or use a local VM
